articles.title: This Is the Rarest Color for National Flags—Can You Guess What It Is?
articles.content:
美国今年夏天迎来了250岁生日，庆祝方式包括燃放烟花、参加游行以及挥舞国旗。为什么国旗如此重要？“地球上几乎每个国家都进化出了自己的国旗，”詹姆斯·费里根说，他是一位旗帜学专家（即旗帜历史学家），也是北美旗帜学协会现任财务主管。“这是他们向世界发出无声信号的机会——‘嘿，我们在这儿！这就是我们！’”

这种被看见的渴望——无论是跨越陆地还是海洋，或是从更哲学的角度——帮助解释了为什么尽管没有法律规定各国必须设计国旗，但每个国家都有。费里根说：“没有人愿意被落下。这几乎像是我们与生俱来的本能。”

但选择一种颜色？这曾是一个难题——至少在一段时间内是。

费里根说：“假设我想把这块布料染成一种新颜色，我该怎么做？”一个选择是人类血液，但最终会褪色。颜料和天然色素紧随其后，但像血液一样，这些涂层持续不了多久。染料改变了旗帜的格局，但事实证明并非所有染料都一样——这就是为什么我们几乎看不到旗帜上出现一种特定的颜色。

我采访了费里根和迈克尔·格林，了解为什么这种颜色在历史上如此稀缺。格林是“美好旗帜”公司的旗帜学专家，这家总部位于印第安纳波利斯的公司生产他们认同的旗帜（并将其部分销售额捐赠给相关事业）。继续阅读，发现最稀有的旗帜颜色——看看你是否猜对了。

信不信由你，是紫色。目前，在近200个国家中，只有5个国家的官方或共同官方旗帜包含紫色。“它极其罕见，”费里根说。这些国家是……

“在1850年代之前，紫色是最难生产的染料，”格林说。“它主要使用海螺制成，即使是少量的染料也需要大量的海螺。”费里根说，你需要大约10,000只这种小型软体动物——它们只分布在现代黎巴嫩沿海——才能染制一件衣服！

1856年，一位18岁的英国化学家意外合成了第一种紫色合成染料，但到那时，国旗的格局已基本固定。“由于大多数国旗是在1850年代之前设计的，紫色根本不是一种实用的颜色，”格林说。

由于古代海螺染料（称为推罗紫）的稀缺性，只有非常富有和有权势的人才能获得。“所以紫色只留给皇室和有能力负担的人，”格林说。即使在紫色染料进入大众视野170年后的今天，紫色仍被世界各地的君主广泛使用，最熟悉的可能是英国王室。

已故的伊丽莎白二世女王和现任的查尔斯三世国王在加冕典礼上穿着深紫色天鹅绒礼服，称为“御礼袍”。伊丽莎白的父亲乔治六世国王和卡米拉王后也穿着紫色礼服，英国帝国王冠也是紫色的。其他国家的皇室，包括日本、泰国和西班牙，也穿着紫色皇家礼服。

“最突出的颜色是红色，”格林说。红色不仅代表为国独立或某项事业而战的“爱国者的鲜血”，他还指出——而且红色是一种易于生产的颜色。“事实上，它出现在超过70%的国旗上，”他说。蓝色位居第二，而白色——可以说是旗帜上最容易使用的颜色——位列第三。

原色之所以占据主导地位，是因为它们容易获得而且可以在当地采购，费里根说。他以丹麦拥有800年历史的国家旗帜为例，这是世界上最古老的国旗，上面有白色十字图案的红色背景。红色可能是用一种叫茜草的植物染料，直到美洲原住民使用的胭脂红虫染料被引入欧洲。木材树皮、花朵被用于其他颜色，而靛蓝（一种深蓝色染料）在16世纪开始传播。

之后，红、白、蓝三色开始在各种组合中大放异彩。费里根说，荷兰人在船上使用这些颜色（之前尝试过橙色但发现不持久），这促使在荷兰学习造船的彼得大帝将这种配色带到俄罗斯。然后还有法国、英国、美国和数十个其他国家的红白蓝旗帜。

“绿色是另一个很少出现在旗帜上的颜色的好例子，有很好的历史原因，”格林说。“它曾被使用过，但暴露在紫外线下时，绿色会很快褪色变成不理想的色调。”这就是荷兰人在17世纪初将旗帜上的橙色换成红色的原因之一（尽管橙色仍是国家颜色，任何观看世界杯的人都可以证实）。

灰色、棕色和粉色也是不太受欢迎的国家旗帜颜色。

紫色有可能成为更常见的旗帜颜色，但这是一个缓慢的过程。今天使用紫色的每个国家国旗都是在染料变得容易获得之后设计的，但各国并不经常更改国旗。“国旗设计在国家层面很少改变，除非政府发生重大动荡，”格林说。“但我们现在看到次国家认同（如州和城市）正在兴起，他们希望利用旗帜的力量来建立身份认同、自豪感和归属感。”

玻利维亚的共同官方国旗维帕拉就是使用紫色的年轻旗帜的一个很好的例子，但它很罕见。“大多数国家国旗都是历史纹章的简化，”格林说。“年轻的国家，包括美国，都遵循其前辈设定的旗帜学传统。”
articles.summary:
The United States turned 250 this summer, and to celebrate, we shot off fireworks and went to parades and, above it all, waved the American flag. Why is that? “Every country on Earth, for the most part, has evolved a national flag,” says James Ferrigan, a vexillologist (aka flag expert and historian) and the current treasurer of the North American Vexillological Association. “It’s kind of their one chance to send a nonverbal message to the world, like, ‘Hey, we’re over here! This is us!'”

That desire to be seen—across the land or ocean, as well as more philosophically—helps explain why, even though there’s no law requiring countries to develop a flag, we all do it. “No one wants to be left out,” he says. “It’s almost like we’re hardwired for it, you know?”

But choosing a color? That posed a problem—for a while, at least.

“Let’s say I want to turn this fabric into a new color,” Ferrigan says. “How do I do it?” One option, human blood, would have eventually worn off. Paint and natural pigments came next, but like blood, these coatings didn’t last long. Dyes changed the flag game considerably, but it turns out not all dyes were created equal—which is why we hardly ever see one particular color on flags.

I talked to Ferrigan and Michael Green, the vexillologist behind Flags for Good, an Indianapolis-based company that makes flags they believe in (and donates a portion of every sale to relevant causes), to learn why this color has been so historically absent. Read on to discover the rarest flag color—and see if you guessed correctly.

Get Reader’s Digest’s Read Up newsletter for more fun facts, cleaning, humor, tech and travel all week long.

What is the rarest color for a national flag?

Believe it or not, it’s purple. Currently, only five countries (out of nearly 200) have purple in their official or co-official flags. “It’s exceedingly rare,” Ferrigan says. And those countries are …

  • Dominica: “You have the Sisserou parrot on Dominica’s,” Ferrigan says. This protected (and purple) parrot, which is the national bird of the Caribbean-island nation and isn’t found anywhere else in the world, is featured prominently on many of the country’s national symbols.
  • Spain: “The lions on Spanish flags are properly called purpure, or morado,” Ferrigan says. “So they’re purple.” (Purpure is a shade of purple common in heraldry, and morado is the Spanish word for “purple.”) Sometimes the lions appear pink, especially on the internet, but purple is the official color used on Spanish coats of arms and flags.
  • Nicaragua: The blue-and-white-striped flag of this Central American country centers its coat of arms, which features a rainbow over volcanoes and a Phrygian cap, a red headpiece that symbolizes liberty and freedom. There is a tiny purple stripe in the rainbow.
  • El Salvador: Like Nicaragua’s flag, El Salvador’s derives from an earlier Federal Republic of Central America flag, with blue and white stripes and the country’s coat of arms. Again, there is a small bit of purple in the center rainbow.
  • Bolivia: There is no missing the purple in the Wiphala, a checkerboard banner with a rainbow motif that is the co-official flag of Bolivia. Adopted in 2009 to honor Indigenous peoples of the Andes region, it flies alongside Bolivia’s traditional tricolor flag on government buildings.

Why do so few countries use purple?

“Until the 1850s, purple was the hardest dye to produce,” Green says. “It was mainly made by using a sea snail, and it took a ton of them to make even a small amount of dye.” Ferrigan says you’d need something like 10,000 of these little mollusks, which were found only off the coast of modern-day Lebanon, to dye a single garment!

In 1856, an 18-year-old British chemist accidentally synthesized the first purple synthetic dye, but by then, the die was cast, so to speak. “Since most national flags were designed before the 1850s, purple was just not a practical color,” Green says. 

What does purple traditionally symbolize, and who uses it?

Because of the rarity of the sea-snail dye (called Tyrian purple) in ancient times, only the very wealthy and powerful had access. “So purple was reserved for royalty and those who could afford it,” Green says. Even today, 170 years after purple dye became available to the masses, purple is widely used by monarchies around the world, perhaps most familiarly by the British royal family.

The late Queen Elizabeth and the current King Charles wore deep purple velvet garments called Robes of Estate at their coronations. Elizabeth’s father, King George VI, and Queen Camilla also wore purple robes, and Britain’s Imperial State Crown is purple. Royal families in other countries, including Japan, Thailand and Spain, also wear purple royal attire.

What is the most common flag color?

“Far and away, red,” says Green. Red not only represents the “blood of the patriots that fought” for their country, independence or a cause, he notes—it’s also an easy color to produce. “In fact, it appears on over 70% of national flags,” he says. Blue comes in second, and white, which is arguably the easiest color to use for a flag, is third.

Primary colors came to dominate because they were readily available and could be acquired locally, Ferrigan says. He gives the example of Denmark’s 800-year-old national flag, the oldest in the world, which has a white cross on a red background. The red was probably a vegetable dye called madder until cochineal, an insect-based dye used by Native Americans, was introduced to Europe. Wood pulp, bark and flowers were used for other colors, and indigo (a deep blue dye) spread in the 16th century.

After that, it was off to the races for red, white and blue—in all kinds of combinations. Ferrigan says the Dutch used these colors on their ships (after trying orange and finding it didn’t last), which prompted Peter the Great, who studied shipbuilding in the Netherlands, to bring the combo to Russia. And then there’s the red, white and blue flags of France, Britain, the U.S and dozens of other countries.

What other flag colors are unpopular?

“Orange is another great example of a color that rarely appears on flags, and for good historical reason,” Green says. “It was used, but when exposed to UV light, the orange colors would fade quickly and turn into undesirable shades.” That’s one reason the Dutch swapped out orange for red on their flag back in the early 17th century (though orange is still the national color, as anyone watching the World Cup can attest).

Gray, brown and pink are also unpopular national flag colors.

Is the prevalence of purple changing?

Potentially, but slowly. Every national flag using purple today was developed after the dye became readily available, but it’s not like countries change their flags often. “Designs rarely change at the national level unless there is a large upheaval in the government,” Green says. “But we now are seeing a surge in subnational identities, like states and cities, moving toward wanting to utilize the power of flags to build identity, pride and belonging.”

The Wiphala, Bolivia’s co-official national flag, is a good example of a young flag using purple, but it’s a rarity. “Most national flags are simplifications of historic coats of arms,” Green says. “Younger nations, the United States included, follow the vexillological traditions set by their predecessors.”

About the experts

  • James Ferrigan is one of the country’s foremost vexillologists. He is the treasurer of the North American Vexillological Association, has written numerous academic papers and advises public and private flag collectors, including the Smithsonian.
  • Michael Green is a vexillologist and the owner of Flags for Good, an Indianapolis-based company that makes flags and donates a portion of the proceeds to charity ($300,000 and counting). Green is also a technical editor of The Complete Guide to Flags of the World, 4th Edition.

Why trust us

At Reader’s Digest, we’re committed to producing high-quality content by writers with expertise and experience in their field in consultation with relevant, qualified experts. We rely on reputable primary sources, including government and professional organizations and academic institutions as well as our writers’ personal experiences where appropriate. We verify all facts and data, back them with credible sourcing and revisit them over time to ensure they remain accurate and up to date. Read more about our team, our contributors and our editorial policies.

Sources:

  • James Ferrigan, vexillological historian and treasurer of the North American Vexillological Association; phone interview, July 9, 2026
  • Michael Green, founder of Flags for Good and technical editor of The Complete Guide to Flags of the World, 4th Edition; email interview, July 10, 2026
  • Government of the Commonwealth of Dominica: “National Bird”
  • Gov.uk: “The Coronation Roll”
  • Royal.uk: “A first glimpse at Their Majesties’ Coronation robes”
  • Science History Institute: “William Henry Perkin”
  • World Population Review: “Countries with red, white and blue flags”

Do You Know the History of the Rainbow Flag?

Strange Places You’ll Find the American Flag

The post This Is the Rarest Color for National Flags—Can You Guess What It Is? appeared first on Reader's Digest.

articles.title: Yikes! Princess Diana Convinced Prince Charles to Get Rid of His Beloved Dog—Here’s What Happened
articles.content:
我们非常喜欢戴安娜王妃，这是真的，但这个故事确实让人难以接受。事实上，关于这位已故威尔士王妃的这个爆料让我们有点心碎，我们至今仍在消化最近公开的这则消息——一封40年前由查尔斯国王写的信在伦敦奇西克拍卖行被拍卖，成交价为656英镑（880美元）。

但让我们从头说起。戴安娜和查尔斯看起来像是天作之合，但正如我们现在所知，他们从一开始就不合适。即使在1981年7月29日结婚之前，他们就已经存在问题。尽管如此，他们确实努力想让这段婚姻走下去，而查尔斯为戴安娜做出了一个巨大的牺牲——在我们看来，这是一个相当极端的举动。他放弃了自己最心爱的伙伴之一——一只名叫哈维的黄色拉布拉多犬，因为戴安娜不喜欢它。（我们懂。）

继续阅读，看看发生了什么——以及戴安娜是否曾经有过“狗狗救赎”的故事。

显然没有！戴安娜在诺福克郡的帕克庄园和北安普敦郡的奥尔索普长大，这里是英格兰中部地区，周围环绕着各种动物，但狗并非她的最爱。相反，这位已故威尔士王妃真正喜欢的是马。14岁时，她爱上了自己的小马“骚动”。后来，她开始学习骑马，经常能看到她支持参加马球比赛的查尔斯王子，以及在皇家阿斯科特赛马会上为骑手们加油。戴安娜还有一只最喜欢的童年猫咪“果酱”，它出现在波兰艺术家阿尼埃拉·帕夫利科夫斯卡创作的家庭肖像画中。虽然我们不想过于沉重，但戴安娜在车祸后被安葬的奥尔索普椭圆形湖中央小岛，曾经是家族宠物的墓地，包括果酱。

据皇室内部人士透露，威尔士王妃认为查尔斯心爱的狗“很臭”。这只拉布拉多犬在1970年代末进入查尔斯的生活，比戴安娜本人还要早，经常陪伴他参加钓鱼之旅。然而，在他们结婚三年后，据报道戴安娜坚持要求哈维离开王室家庭。

考虑到查尔斯是多么爱狗的人——2025年他迎来了最新的小狗斯纳夫，一只可爱的意大利水猎犬——我们很难想象他不得不与自己的伙伴告别时有多伤心。在这封现已公开的信中，他形容哈维是“一个出色的家伙”，它“滑稽古怪的把戏”让他“笑个不停”。

当戴安娜坚持要送走哈维时，查尔斯确保它去了最好的新家。1984年，他把哈维送给了当时威尔士亲王和王妃的管家菲利普·克里西中校及其妻子。

令人悲伤的是，这只狗不得不在1987年12月被实施安乐死。查尔斯在信中哀悼他“忠诚的老朋友”，说他“完全赞同做出这个决定……虽然这总是一个极其艰难的决定”。然后他补充说，他“非常高兴”哈维的新家人“如此爱他”，并感谢他的员工“非常好心地同意照顾他，给他一个远离那些潮湿狗舍的快乐退休生活”。

好吧，没有人（目前）透露哈维事件是否在已经问题重重的两人之间造成了另一道裂痕，但我们认为可以肯定地说，查尔斯对送走自己心爱的狗并不高兴。毕竟，他过去是（现在也是）一个超级爱狗的人。但众所周知，皇室成员总是尽可能把分歧关在门后解决，所以我们可能永远不知道在查尔斯妥协之前到底发生了什么。

令人惊讶的是，是的！戴安娜和查尔斯后来迎来了两只杰克罗素梗犬，蒂格和鲁，来到了这个家庭。而且，据戴安娜的前管家保罗·伯雷尔说，威廉和哈里小时候都“迫不及待地想和它们玩耍”。伯雷尔还透露，戴安娜在和查尔斯离婚后仍然是爱狗人士。据报道，她计划在1997年夏天买一只金色拉布拉多犬，但遗憾的是，这从未实现，随后她在当年8月31日去世。

是的，查尔斯和卡米拉都是爱狗人士，就像已故的伊丽莎白二世女王（还记得她著名的柯基犬吗？）一样，他们目前养了三只狗。卡米拉是伦敦巴特西猫狗之家的大使和赞助人，2017年从该机构领养了两只杰克罗素梗犬——蓝铃和贝丝，它们 famously featured on Camilla's coronation gown.

令人悲伤的是，贝丝于2024年11月去世，但卡米拉在2025年11月从同一家救援机构领养了一只名叫莫利的混血犬。此外还有斯纳夫——查尔斯20多年来的第一只狗——也在2025年加入了这个家庭。与其他狗不同的是，这只小狗是私人赠送的，不是一只救援犬。据报道，国王对这只意大利水猎犬“非常着迷”，他们俩都热爱采蘑菇。这听起来像是真正的（小狗）爱情！

这篇文章《天哪！戴安娜王妃说服查尔斯王子送走他心爱的狗——来看看发生了什么》首次发表于《读者文摘》。
articles.summary:
We absolutely adore Princess Diana, we really do, but this story is a tough one to swallow. In fact, this revelation about the late Princess of Wales broke our hearts a little, and we’re still processing the news that came to light when a 40-year-old letter written by King Charles was recently auctioned off by Chiswick Auctions in London for £656 ($880).

But let’s start from the beginning. Diana and Charles seemed like the perfect couple on paper, but as we now know, they were a mismatch from Day One. Even before they tied the knot on July 29, 1981, they were having problems. That being said, they really tried to make it work, and Charles made a huge sacrifice for Diana—and, if you ask us, quite a drastic one. He gave up one of his absolute favorite companions, a yellow Labrador retriever named Harvey, because Diana wasn’t a fan. (We know.)

Read on to find out what happened—and whether Diana ever embarked on a puppy-redemption arc.

Get Reader’s Digest’s Read Up newsletter for more royals, pets, humor, cleaning, travel, tech and fun facts all week long.

Was Princess Diana a dog person?

Apparently not! Diana grew up at Park House, Sandringham, and later Althorp in Northamptonshire, in the English Midlands, surrounded by animals, but dogs weren’t her favorites. Instead, the late Princess of Wales adored horses. As a 14-year-old, she fell in love with her pony Scuffle. Later, she took riding lessons and could often be spotted supporting Prince Charles at polo matches and cheering on the riders at the Royal Ascot races.

Diana also had a favorite childhood cat named Marmalade, who made an appearance in a family portrait by Polish artist Aniela Pawlikowska. And while we don’t want to get too morbid, the island in the middle of the Oval Lake at Althorp, where Diana was buried after her tragic car accident, was a former cemetery for family pets, including Marmalade.

Why didn’t Diana like Harvey?

According to royal insiders, the Princess of Wales thought Charles’s beloved dog was “smelly.” The Lab had come into Charles’s life in the late 1970s, before Diana herself, and had frequently accompanied him on fishing trips. However, three years into their marriage, Diana reportedly insisted that Harvey should leave the royal household.

Considering how much of a dog person Charles is—he welcomed his latest puppy, Snuff, an adorable Lagotto Romagnolo, in 2025—we can’t imagine how heartbroken he must have been to say goodbye to his buddy. In the letter that has now become public, he described Harvey as “a splendid character” that made him “laugh so much by his dotty antics.”

What happened to Harvey?

When Diana insisted on getting rid of Harvey, Charles made sure he went to the best possible new home. In 1984, he gave Harvey to Lieutenant Colonel Phillip Creasy, the comptroller of the Prince and Princess of Wales at the time, and his wife.

Sadly, the dog had to be put to sleep in December 1987. Mourning his “loyal old friend,” Charles said in the letter that Creasy was “absolutely right to take the decision … although it is always a desperate one to have to take.” He then added that he was “so pleased” Harvey’s new family “loved him so much” and thanked his employee for his “great kindness in agreeing to look after him and for giving him such a happy retirement away from those damp kennels.”

Did this cause tension between the couple?

Well, nobody has (yet) spilled the beans about whether the Harvey situation caused another rift between the already-troubled pair, but we think it’s safe to say that Charles wasn’t thrilled about giving away his beloved dog. After all, he was (and still is) a huge dog lover. But as we know, the royals tend to keep their disagreements behind closed doors whenever possible, so we may never know what actually happened before Charles gave in.

Did Diana ever come around and become a dog person?

Surprisingly, yes! Diana and Charles later welcomed two Jack Russell terriers, Tigger and Roo, to the family. And, according to Diana’s former butler Paul Burrell, both William and Harry were “eager to play” with them as kids. Burrell also revealed that Diana remained a dog person even after her divorce from Charles. She reportedly planned to buy a golden Lab in the summer of 1997, but sadly, this never happened, and then she died on Aug. 31 of that year.

Does Charles have dogs now?

Yes, Charles and Camilla are huge dog lovers, just like Queen Elizabeth II (remember her famous corgis?) and currently have three of them. Camilla, a longtime ambassador and patron of the Battersea Dogs and Cats Home in London, adopted two Jack Russells from the organization in 2017—Bluebell and Beth, who were famously featured on Camilla’s coronation gown.

Sadly, Beth passed away in November 2024, but Camilla adopted a mixed breed named Moley from the same rescue in November 2025. And then there’s Snuff, Charles’s first dog in more than 20 years, who joined the family in 2025 as well. Unlike the others, the pup was a private gift, not a rescue dog. The king was reportedly “besotted” with the Lagotto Romagnolo, and they both share a love for mushroom foraging. It sounds like true (puppy) love!
